@Mirandataps Please explain Zorn's lemma in the simplest way possible.
@causticshota Ordinals represent ordering. Specifically, well-ordering, where you can't have an infinite list of ordinals going downwards in order infinitely.

@causticshota Ordinals are usually represented as the set of all lower ordinals, which is useful since they can have a one-to-one correspondence to cardinals. aleph_0 is usually considered to be w as w is the smallest ordinal that can have a one-to-one correspondence to aleph_0.
@causticshota In essence, cardinals describe the size of something, while ordinals describe the order of something. Ordinals tend to pop up where you have infinite hierarchies.
@causticshota The most obvious example is Googology, where fast-growing functions end up outgrowing an infinite amount of smaller ones, and functions must be indexed using ordinals.

@pickover I paid $0 to have a complete, deep, and profound understanding of this object and its history. 

The 24-cell or icosatetrachoron has Schläfli symbol {3,4,3} which indicates there are 3 octahedra to a vertex. (1/n)

@pickover The Schläfli symbol also tells us it is self-dual, as the Schläfli symbol can be reversed without changing the order of the digits. (A dual of a polytope is the reverse of its Schläfli symbol.) (2/n)

@pickover The vertex figure (the shape produced by cutting a vertex perpendicularly to a straight line from the center of the solid) is a cube, each face corresponding with the vertex figure of each octahedron on that vertex. (3/n)

@pickover The icosatetrachoron is unique as it is the only self-dual regular convex (spherical) polytope (n-dimensional shape with flat sides) which is not an n-simplex (analogue of the triangle in various dimensions). (4/n)

Ok so I see orcs trending and its about racism. I take a look at the description of orcs Twitter was being all riled up for. At first glance, it seems to have a pretty racist attitude which I found problematic. Then I looked at what others were saying... 1/n

People were saying people were comparing orcs with black people. I found this pretty racist from a face-value perspective, comparing black people to a bloodthirsty race, but from a meta perspective, it seemed similar to descriptions of poc as barbaric... 2/n

Then I realized no one actually said black people are like orcs. So I think the problem is that the description of orcs is similar to how historically, people would describe poc like black people, so it promotes a racist attitude... 3/n

I think the main issue though is that orcs are in a strange place where they are bloodthirsty and barbaric but at the same time, they are humanoid and capable of empathy and love, in contrast to say, goblins in Goblin Slayer, which lack any empathy... 4/n

Also, I don't think Tolkien should have any bearing since this is DND but he stated this:
"[Orcs] are (or were) squat, broad, flat-nosed, sallow-skinned, with wide mouths and slant eyes; in fact degraded and repulsive versions of the (to Europeans) least lovely Mongol-types." 5/n
So if y'all are complaining about comparing orcs to black people and also taking what Tolkien says seriously, y'all should complain about this. 6/n

That being said Tolkien seemed to be mocking racists in that quote. And there is somewhat of a difference, one compares orcs to black people because of the similarities of how black people were historically described and orcs... 7/n

Another thing is that people are angry because racism was justified by stuff like "They aren't human, they're savages, so it's ok to discriminate against them" which sounds a lot like the description of orcs Twitter got riled up for... 8/n
@AllEliteAshley I honestly don't see how the other one being created by a TERF and a racist matters. Separate art from artist. That being said, the new one is better.
People are riled up because attitudes towards orcs echo that of attitudes towards POC, especially troubling since orcs do seem racially coded. 9/n

People who say that viewing orcs as POC is on the person who viewed them as such aren't considering that orcs are racially coded to be like Africans, American Indians, Mongols, etc. and also aren't considering the similar attitudes towards orcs as POC historically. 10/n

@serbantanasa @repligate @AnActualWizard @birdhustle assuming many world hypothesis, there is no logical uncertainty about there being a universe where an electron collapses to a specific position, but there is uncertainty about your location in the multiverse, specifically whether you're in that universe, hence "indexical"

@serbantanasa @repligate @AnActualWizard @birdhustle btw "indexical" basically means dependent on context. the word "you" is indexical because it refers to different things depending on who you are. "next Thursday" is indexical because it depends on when you're speaking.

@serbantanasa @repligate @AnActualWizard @birdhustle logical uncertainty comes from not knowing the proportion of universes where that electron collapses into that position, indexical uncertainty comes from not knowing which individual universe you'll end up in even knowing the actual proportion of universes
@birdhustle @AnActualWizard @serbantanasa @repligate the many worlds hypothesis is more specific than there being multiple universes for each wave-function collapse

@birdhustle @AnActualWizard @serbantanasa @repligate should really be called the decoherence hypothesis since it has less to do with multiple universes and more to do with the fact that wave function collapse looks like decoherence from the perspective of a decohered particle

@birdhustle @AnActualWizard @serbantanasa @repligate framed like this, wave-function collapse seems like a redundant hypothesis to explain a thing that we already know about. it also returns continuous-ness, CPT symmetry and determinism to physics which is nice because everything else is continuous, CPT symmetric, and deterministic

@birdhustle @AnActualWizard @serbantanasa @repligate that seems like a matter of semantics. the implication is still that there's a universe where you chose to get pizza instead of burgers because the quantum RNG you used output a different result.

@ArgonGruber @kindgracekind The only difference between this decision theory, called Son-of-CDT, and functional decision theory is that FDT acts as if this self-modification occured before time.

@kindgracekind The idea is that CDT isn't a good decision theory because it instantly turns into another decision theory, Son-of-CDT. Functional decision theory solves this by acting as if it was a Son-of-CDT for whom the self-modification occured before time.
@ArgonGruber @kindgracekind After the coin flip, maybe, but before the coin flip it's higher EV to turn yourself into Son-of-CDT than to remain as CDT.

@getjonwithit @Husam_Ism well, even if you give it a seemingly innocuous goal like "make paperclips" it won't care about humans because that's not part of its goals. if it wanted to make the earth into a giant paperclip factory (which would be at great cost to humanity) then it would.
@getjonwithit @Husam_Ism notably, it isn't stupid, it knows full well its goals are different than what we intended for it to do. it just doesn't care because why would it?

@getjonwithit @Husam_Ism of course, we can specify goals that are closer to what we want, but any degree to which it's different from what we actually want is a degree to which it's willing to throw away what we want for what we technically specified.

@getjonwithit @Husam_Ism i think your argument depends on assuming that intelligence has little to do with whether an entity will actually affect the world, or at least the parts of the world we care about.

@getjonwithit @Husam_Ism the problem is that intelligence makes one more competent at affecting the world and most goals, especially ones that would be specified by humans, would intersect with parts of the world we care about.

@getjonwithit @Husam_Ism if the AI needs space or matter or energy for anything, then there's a bunch of it right here, and it doesn't care if harvesting it means rendering the earth on which we live uninhabitable (e.g. blocking the sun with a dyson sphere)

@getjonwithit @Husam_Ism with COVID-19, its stupidity means it can't build dyson spheres, and more importantly, it means that it can't fight against our counterplays, like vaccines and social distancing.

@Wenitte1 @pachabelcanon but regardless, let's say you send a spaceship into the event horizon of a black hole, or have it move so fast to so far away that the expansion of the universe prevents light from coming back to you from the spaceship

@Wenitte1 @pachabelcanon does the ship still exist? whether it continues to exist or whether it just magically disintegrates behind the event horizon doesn't materially affect you in any way because not even light is fast enough to reach you. in fact, whether the spaceship exists or not is unfalsifiable.

@Wenitte1 @pachabelcanon this is an argument that falsification is not the end-all-be-all. in cases like this we use Occam's razor and assume that the spaceship continues to follow the usual laws of physics rather than magically disappearing.

@Wenitte1 @pachabelcanon likewise, people argue for the many worlds interpretation because they think it is simpler and requires less assumptions than the alternative, and that therefore Occam's razor favors it. whether this is true or not is another discussion.
@Wenitte1 @pachabelcanon also regardless of whether many worlds is falsifiable or not, i think at least the existence of objective wave function collapse is falsifiable

@halogen1048576 @norabelrose the copenhagen interpretation requires the existence of a physical process called collapse in addition to states evolving according to the schroedinger equation. in contrast, many worlds says things evolve according to only the schroedinger equation, no collapse necessary.

@halogen1048576 @norabelrose on that note, this means that many worlds *does* actually have testable predictions, namely that what the copenhagen interpretation calls collapse is a continuous, differentiable process rather than a discontinuous process as ci posits

@halogen1048576 @norabelrose the reason that the many worlds interpretation is called an interpretation rather than a theory is because at the time people weren't quite clear on the difference and the name stuck.

@halogen1048576 @norabelrose for that reason i tend to favor calling the many worlds interpretation the everettian interpretation. the part of it where it suggests the existence of alternate worlds is not really whats important about it

@halogen1048576 @jayendra_ram @norabelrose the math is different though, they do actually make different predictions. the copenhagen interpretation suggests that the process of collapse is discontinuous while many world suggests it is continuous and differentiable

@robertwrighter @ToFollowBrights @norabelrose this is not *quite* what is happening. it's more like some worlds are associated with negative numbers and others are associated with positive numbers and sometimes you can arrange it so that worlds with results you don't want add and cancel out, leaving only the results you want

@robertwrighter @ToFollowBrights @norabelrose this is another reason why many worlds is attractive, because other interpretations imply that some computations are carried out on hardware that *isn't real* where many worlds says they merely happen in other worlds.
@robertwrighter @ToFollowBrights @norabelrose quantum bayesianism gets ahead of the curve by saying nothing is real.
@ToFollowBrights @robertwrighter @norabelrose which is why i said "like" and not "is"
